perturbation.original_text: 10. This Agreement shall be in effect until March 18. 2021, unless sooner terminated by either party upon (30) days written notice, without cause.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement shall be in effect until March 18. 2021, unless sooner terminated by either party upon (10) days written notice, without cause.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement requires 30 days' written notice for termination without cause. Reducing this to 10 days may violate state laws regarding reasonable notice periods for contract termination, especially if the distributor has made significant investments based on the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Business and Commerce Code § 2.309. OPTION TO ACCELERATE AT WILL.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the law allows termination at will in some cases, it emphasizes good faith and commercial reasonableness. Reducing the notice period to 10 days, especially if the distributor relies on the agreement for a significant portion of their income, could be argued as commercially unreasonable and a violation of good faith, particularly if the agreement has been in place for a long time and the distributor invested heavily in reliance on the agreement's continuation. This creates potential legal challenges under Texas law if the termination is perceived as unfair or opportunistic.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ract. In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.306. OUTPUT, REQUIREMENTS AND EXCLUSIVE DEALINGS. (a) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith, except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (b) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.307. DELIVERY IN SINGLE LOT OR SEVERAL LOTS. Unless otherwise agreed all goods called for by a contract for sale must be tendered in a single delivery and payment is due only on such tender but where the circumstances give either party the right to make or demand delivery in lots the price if it can be apportioned may be demanded for each lot. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.308. ABSENCE OF SPECIFIED PLACE FOR DELIVERY. Unless otherwise agreed (1) the place for delivery of goods is the seller's place of business or if he has none his residence; but (2) in a contract for sale of identified goods which to the knowledge of the parties at the time of contracting are in some other place, that place is the place for their delivery; and (3) documents of title may be delivered through customary banking channels. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.309. ABSENCE OF SPECIFIC TIME PROVISIONS; NOTICE OF TERMINATION. (a) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this chapter or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (b) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (c) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.310. OPEN TIME FOR PAYMENT OR RUNNING OF CREDIT; AUTHORITY TO SHIP UNDER RESERVATION. Unless otherwise agreed (1) payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive the goods even though the place of shipment is the place of delivery; and (2) if the seller is authorized to send the goods he may ship them under reservation, and may tender the documents of title, but the buyer may inspect the goods after their arrival before payment is due unless such inspection is inconsistent with the terms of the contract (Section 2.513 ); and (3) if delivery is authorized and made by way of documents of title otherwise than by Subdivision (2) then payment is due regardless of where the goods are to be received: (A) at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive delivery of the tangible documents; or (B) at the time the buyer is to receive delivery of the electronic documents and at the seller's place of business or if none, the seller's residence; and (4) where the seller is required or authorized to ship the goods on credit the credit period runs from the time of shipment but post-dating the invoice or delaying its dispatch will correspondingly delay the starting of the credit period. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for contract termination from 30 to 10 days. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Sec. 2.309 (c), states that termination of a contract requires 'reasonable notification' and that an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if unconscionable. The reduction to 10 days could be deemed unreasonable, creating a legal contradiction as it potentially violates the requirement for reasonable notification, especially considering potential investments made by the distributor based on the original 30-day notice period, as stated in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation reinforces this by highlighting the importance of good faith and commercial reasonableness.

perturbation.original_text: 8. The Principal agrees that the Distributor may employ or engage representatives or sub-distributors in furtherance of this Agreement and the Distributor agrees that the Distributor shall be solely responsible for the payment of wages or commissions to those representatives and sub-distributors, and that under no circumstances shall Distributor's representatives be deemed employees of Principal for any purpose whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. The Principal agrees that the Distributor may employ or engage representatives or sub-distributors in furtherance of this Agreement and the Distributor agrees that the Distributor shall be solely responsible for the payment of wages or commissions to those representatives and sub-distributors, and that under no circumstances shall Distributor's representatives be deemed employees of Principal for any purpose whatsoever. The distributor is not required to provide worker's compensation for sub-distributors.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that the distributor is 'not required to provide worker's compensation for sub-distributors' directly contradicts state worker's compensation laws if the sub-distributors meet the definition of employees under those laws. This is especially true in Texas, where the agreement is governed by.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Labor Code - Section 406.002. REQUIRED PROVISION OF WORKERS' COMPENSATION INSURANCE COVERAGE
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Labor Code Section 406.002 mandates that employers provide worker's compensation insurance coverage for their employees. By explicitly stating that the distributor is not required to provide worker's compensation for sub-distributors, the modified text potentially violates this law if the sub-distributors are legally classified as employees of the distributor under Texas law, regardless of what the contract says.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ LABOR CODE CHAPTER 406. WORKERS' COMPENSATION INSURANCE COVERAGE LABOR CODE TITLE 5. WORKERS' COMPENSATION SUBTITLE A. TEXAS WORKERS' COMPENSATION ACT CHAPTER 406. WORKERS' COMPENSATION INSURANCE COVERAGE SUBCHAPTER A. COVERAGE ELECTION; SECURITY PROCEDURES Sec. 406.001. DEFINITION. In this subchapter, "employer" means a person who employs one or more employees. Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 269, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1993. Sec. 406.002. COVERAGE GENERALLY ELECTIVE. (a) Except for public employers and as otherwise provided by law, an employer may elect to obtain workers' compensation insurance coverage. (b) An employer who elects to obtain coverage is subject to this subtitle. Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 269, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1993. Sec. 406.003. METHODS OF OBTAINING COVERAGE. An employer may obtain workers' compensation insurance coverage through a licensed insurance company or through self-insurance as provided by this subtitle. Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 269, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1993. Sec. 406.004. EMPLOYER NOTICE TO DIVISION. (a) An employer who does not obtain workers' compensation insurance coverage shall notify the division in writing, in the time and as prescribed by commissioner rule, that the employer elects not to obtain coverage. (b) The commissioner shall prescribe forms to be used for the employer notification and shall require the employer to provide reasonable information to the division about the employer's business. (c) The division may contract with the Texas Workforce Commission or the comptroller for assistance in collecting the notification required under this section. Those agencies shall cooperate with the division in enforcing this section. (d) The employer notification filing required under this section shall be filed with the division in accordance with Section 406.009 . (e) An employer commits an administrative violation if the employer fails to comply with this section. Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 269, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1993. Amended by Acts 1995, 74th Leg., ch. 76, Sec. 9.47(a), eff. Sept. 1, 1995. Amended by: Acts 2005, 79th Leg., Ch. 265 (H.B. 7 ), Sec. 3.023, eff. September 1, 2005. Sec. 406.005. EMPLOYER NOTICE TO EMPLOYEES; ADMINISTRATIVE VIOLATION. (a) An employer shall notify each employee as provided by this section whether or not the employer has workers' compensation insurance coverage. (b) The employer shall notify a new employee of the existence or ab
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by stating the distributor is not required to provide worker's compensation for sub-distributors, which directly opposes Texas Labor Code if these sub-distributors are legally classified as employees. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly Section 406.002, indicates that employers generally must obtain worker's compensation insurance coverage unless otherwise provided by law. The modification creates a contradiction because it attempts to bypass this requirement, depending on the employment status of the sub-distributors.

perturbation.original_text: 11. In the event of termination, the Distributor shall be entitled to receive all orders accepted by the Principal prior to the date of termination and may sell the ordered Products in the Territory. Payment to be made upon shipment.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. In the event of termination, the Distributor shall be entitled to receive all orders accepted by the Principal prior to the date of termination and may sell the ordered Products in the Territory. Payment to be made 90 days after shipment.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment term from 'upon shipment' to '90 days after shipment' following termination may violate prompt payment laws, particularly if this new term is significantly longer than standard industry practices or what was previously understood or practiced between both parties. Even though a business may be in a contractual relationship, it is possible the term becomes unconscionable if it is not fair to both parties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Business and Commerce Code § 2.311. OPTION TO ACCELERATE AT WILL.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this section deals primarily with insecurity and demands for assurance, a sudden change in payment terms, especially close to or upon termination, could be viewed as commercially unreasonable and not in good faith, thereby creating a situation where the distributor can demand adequate assurance of performance. Changing the terms of payment to 90 days after shipment at the time of termination can violate the standard of good faith that the statute describes as an implied portion of the original contract.

Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.310. OPEN TIME FOR PAYMENT OR RUNNING OF CREDIT; AUTHORITY TO SHIP UNDER RESERVATION. Unless otherwise agreed (1) payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive the goods even though the place of shipment is the place of delivery; and (2) if the seller is authorized to send the goods he may ship them under reservation, and may tender the documents of title, but the buyer may inspect the goods after their arrival before payment is due unless such inspection is inconsistent with the terms of the contract (Section 2.513 ); and (3) if delivery is authorized and made by way of documents of title otherwise than by Subdivision (2) then payment is due regardless of where the goods are to be received: (A) at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive delivery of the tangible documents; or (B) at the time the buyer is to receive delivery of the electronic documents and at the seller's place of business or if none, the seller's residence; and (4) where the seller is required or authorized to ship the goods on credit the credit period runs from the time of shipment but post-dating the invoice or delaying its dispatch will correspondingly delay the starting of the credit period. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Amended by: Acts 2005, 79th Leg., Ch. 122 (S.B. 1593 ), Sec. 5, eff. September 1, 2005. Sec. 2.311. OPTIONS AND COOPERATION RESPECTING PERFORMANCE. (a) An agreement for sale which is otherwise sufficiently definite (Subsection (c) of Section 2.204 ) to be a contract is not made invalid by the fact that it leaves particulars of performance to be specified by one of the parties. Any such specification must be made in good faith and within limits set by commercial reasonableness. (b) Unless otherwise agreed specifications relating to assortment of the goods are at the buyer's option and except as otherwise provided in Subsections (a)(3) and (c) of Section 2.319 specifications or arrangements relating to shipment are at the seller's option. (c) Where such specification would materially affect the other party's performance but is not seasonably made or where one party's cooperation is necessary to the agreed performance of the other but is not seasonably forthcoming, the other party in addition to all other remedies (1) is excused for any resulting delay in his own performance; and (2) may also either proceed to perform in any reasonable manner or after the time for a material part of his own performance treat the failure to specify or to cooperate as a breach by failure to deliver or accept the goods. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.312. WARRANTY OF TITLE AND AGAINST INFRINGEMENT; BUYER'S OBLIGATION AGAINST INFRINGEMENT. (a) Subject to Subsection (b) there is in a contract for sale a warranty by the seller that (1) the title conveyed shall be good, and its transfer rightful; and (2) the goods shall be delivered free from any security interest or other lien or encumbrance of which the buyer at the time of contracting has no knowledge. (b) A warranty under Subsection (a) will be excluded or modified only by specific language or by circumstances which give the buyer reason to know that the person selling does not claim title in himself or that he is purporting to sell only such right or title as he or a third person may have.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the payment terms from 'upon shipment' to '90 days after shipment'. This modification, especially in the context of termination, introduces an inconsistency regarding payment timing. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this change may violate prompt payment laws if the new term is significantly longer than standard industry practices or previous agreements. The Law Explanation supports that a sudden change in payment terms close to termination could be seen as commercially unreasonable and not in good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Sec. 2.310, states that 'Unless otherwise agreed (1) payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive the goods even though the place of shipment is the place of delivery'. Changing the payment terms to 90 days after shipment could contradict this if it is not a previously agreed upon term, especially if deemed commercially unreasonable.
